retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: The toxin is then endocytosed, where it is acidified. The transmembrane domain is then inserted into the membrane, where it allows the delivery of the catalytic domain to its cytosol. The catalytic domain catalyzes the NAD+ dependent ribosylation of elongation factor 2 and hence inhibits protein synthesis. [9] Although the bacteria get a toxin-producing gene from bacteriophage, however, its regulation is controlled by the bacteria. The diphtheria toxin repressor gene (DtxR) is present on the bacterial chromosome, and toxin production depends upon the expression of tox and bacterial iron metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: The production of toxins is the major virulence factor involved in the pathogenesis of the disease. The gene responsible for the synthesis of the toxin is encoded by corynebacteriophage, which, when integrated into the bacterial genome, can potentially transform the non-toxigenic strain into a virulent toxigenic strain.

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The involvement of multiple subspecialties, including cardiologists, nephrologists, neurologists, infectious disease specialists, surgeons, is necessary to improve the prognosis of the disease. Likewise, physiotherapists and rehabilitation therapists play a crucial role in the recovery of a patient with neurological complications. Ensuring adequate immunization status by a provider can serve as effective prevention from contracting the disease in the first place, which can significantly reduce the burden of disease in a population. Nontoxigenic strains of C. diphtheria , though previously considered as non-virulent, now emerges as an essential cause of endocarditis and pharyngitis. Hence the providers must diagnose and treat the condition at the earliest.

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: It is made up of a single polypeptide chain consisting of 535 amino acids. [3] Further analysis of the toxins via X-ray crystallography along with genetic and biochemical analysis reveals that it is composed of three functional domains, which are Catalytic domain with N–terminal ADP ribosyl transferase activity A transmembrane domain assists in the delivery of catalytic domain across the cell membrane Cell receptor binding domain. After binding of the diphtheria exotoxin to the host cell receptor, it undergoes receptor-mediated endocytosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Still, the toxigenic strains of these bacteria do produce toxins that are then released into the circulation resulting wide range of clinical manifestations. Exotoxin is released by endosomes, causes a localized inflammatory reaction followed by necrosis and tissue destruction. The toxin is made of two joined proteins. The B fragment binds to a receptor on the surface of susceptible host cells, which cleaves the membrane lipid layer enabling segment A to enter. [6] Fragment A inhibits an amino acid transfer, thus inhibiting protein synthesis. Diphtheria toxin (DT) causes a catalytic transfer of NAD to diphthamide, which inactivates the elongation factor that results in protein synthesis blockade and, ultimately, cell death. [7] Local tissue destruction enables the toxin to be carried by blood and lymph to other parts of the body. Elaboration of the diphtheria toxin may affect the myocardium, kidneys, and nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The mode of transmission is via respiratory droplets or contact transmission from an infected host or their carrier. It can also be transmitted by direct contact with the objects or secretions previously in touch with the infected person and or its carrier. After gaining access to the host, the bacteria usually colonize in the upper respiratory tract. They typically do not invade tissue to cause disseminated bacteremia.

retrieved_docs.title: Diphtheria -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Corynebacterium diphtheriae, a nonencapsulated, nonmotile, aerobic gram-positive bacillus, is the bacterium responsible for causing diphtheria. The primary risk factor for contracting this disease is the absence of immunization. While individuals may carry the bacterium asymptomatically, it often impacts the respiratory and integumentary systems. Humans serve as the exclusive hosts for this organism, which resides in the upper respiratory tract, and transmission of the bacteria occurs through airborne droplets.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Pharyngitis -- Pathophysiology -- Corynebacterium diphtheria
retrieved_docs.content: Diphtheria symptoms result from local respiratory tract inflammation and the effects of exotoxin both locally and systemically. The toxin binds to cell membrane receptors and disrupts protein synthesis by inactivating elongation factor 2, leading to cell death.

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The overall mortality of diphtheria has been relatively constant, 10% in the past 100 years. [15] The primary cause of death in patients with diphtheria is due to cardiac complications, where the incidence of developing cardiac complications following diphtheria is 10 to 30%. Still, of those affected, the associated mortality is approximately 50%. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Apart from toxigenic strains of Corynebacterium diphtheria , the nontoxigenic strains though previously considered as nonvirulent, has now been reported to cause infection such as pharyngitis, sepsis, endocarditis, and osteomyelitis. [11] Hence a proper mechanism needs to work in place to identify and adequately treat these conditions effectively. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Extensive implementation of a universal immunization program proved to be effective in declining the incidence of diphtheria to approximately 70% by 1980. [1] Despite there has been some occurrence of frequent outbursts of disease in various parts of the world. The most notable outbreaks that occurred in the post-vaccination era were in several states of the former Soviet Union, with reports of more than 157000 cases and around 5000 deaths. [2] C diphtheria outbreaks in Latvia during 2000-2009 reported the highest incidence rate annually in the European region with a 10-year incidence rate of 23.8 cases per million person-years. [4] About 74% of the case-patient, including 93% of infants who died, were found to be unvaccinated. [4] This highlights the necessity for the universal immunization program.

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of Corynebacterium diphtheria. Outline the epidemiology of Corynebacterium diphtheria. Summarize the clinical findings associated with Corynebacterium diphtheria. Review the importance of improving care coordination amongst interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients with Corynebacterium diphtheria. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: An active immunization schedule against the toxigenic strain of C. diphtheria may have effectively control the toxigenic strain. However, the non-toxigenic strain of C. diphtheria is still at large and has now been an emerging burden of disease in developed countries. In the late 1990s, a severe invasive infection caused by nontoxigenic strains was reported in Europe, particularly in Italy, France, Switzerland, Great Britain, Canada, and Brazil. Among these countries, the most dramatic increase in the number of cases was seen in England and Wales, where the cases of non-toxigenic strain raised from 1 to 294 in the 2000s and have been increasing ever since.

retrieved_docs.title: Diphtheria -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The organism's pathogenicity hinges on the production of exotoxins, which can lead to severe complications, primarily affecting the heart muscle and nervous system. Timely diagnosis plays a pivotal role in achieving favorable clinical outcomes, as initiating antitoxin therapy and appropriate antibiotics early on can significantly impact patient prognosis. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Function -- Generalized Transduction
retrieved_docs.content: In generalized transduction, random pieces of bacterial genomic DNA are packaged inside phage capsids instead of phage genomic DNA as the host cell disintegrates from lytic replication. Should the phage carrying this bacterial DNA inject it into a healthy host cell, it may integrate into the chromosome of that bacterium, altering its genome and that of its daughter cells.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1451
retrieved_docs.content: Corynebacterium diphtheriae, the bacterium responsible for diphtheria, is also associated with other upper respiratory infections, indicating that infection with this organism may coexist with or predispose to additional respiratory pathogens, a relationship that is important for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive management of respiratory illness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1792
retrieved_docs.content: Corynebacterium diphtheriae, the bacterium that causes diphtheria, is associated with other upper respiratory infections, and it produces the diphtheria toxin (difterický toxin), which serves as a causative agent of those additional respiratory infections. Recognizing the connection between Corynebacterium diphtheriae, its toxin, and the broader range of upper respiratory infections is important for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 76
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a member of the combined bacterial and viral vaccines group, meaning it incorporates antigens that protect against both bacterial infections (such as diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis) and viral infections (such as poliomyelitis). By belonging to this category of combination vaccines, the DTPP vaccine enables simultaneous immunization against multiple pathogens, streamlining vaccination schedules and improving patient adherence to preventive care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1613
retrieved_docs.content: Corynebacterium diphtheriae, biotin, the esophagus (ezofágus), immunoglobulins and kyselina chenodeoxycholová (chenodeoxycholic acid) are all identified as entities that participate in or are relevant to metabolism, and metabolism itself is linked to the processing of peptidy (peptides). In addition to its metabolic relevance, Corynebacterium diphtheriae is associated with other upper respiratory infections, highlighting its role in respiratory disease. Clinically, the esophagus (ezofágus) is the direct procedural site for an upper gastrointestinal x‑ray, connecting anatomical imaging with the broader metabolic context of these substances.

Its activity against these Gram‑negative bacteria makes it an appropriate therapeutic option for severe or resistant infections involving either organism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified as a therapeutic intervention and is a subcategory of the broader group termed Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This relationship indicates that preventive vaccination against bacterial pathogens is considered part of the overall therapeutic strategies that include various forms of immunization, injectable treatments, and perfusion techniques, highlighting its role within comprehensive patient care aimed at preventing infection.

This classification links the two agents to the broader group of aminoglycoside antibiotics, indicating that they share pharmacologic characteristics typical of this antimicrobial category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2568
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is identified as a therapeutic intervention that includes immunization, injections, and perfusion methods. This specific preventive measure is placed under the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, indicating that it has a parent relationship to that general class. The overarching class of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions to classify and organize these preventive immunization strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2100
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases are both classified under the broader category of Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This parent category encompasses medical procedures that deliver therapeutic agents directly into the body, either through injection or infusion, as well as preventive measures such as bacterial vaccinations. By grouping these specific interventions within the same therapeutic framework, clinicians recognize that both the administration of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and the use of bacterial vaccines are integral components of immunization and perfusion strategies aimed at preventing disease and delivering treatment effectively.
